title: Voltage Divider for Safer Loads
learningObjectives: Build a two-resistor voltage divider in series
Relate total resistance to circuit current
Explain how resistance ratios set intermediate voltages
introText: Voltage dividers use two resistors in series to split voltage between them. You'll size the divider to pull only a few milliamps so it can feed delicate sensors.

steps.instructionText: Add a 9V source and two resistors in series. Wire them so current flows through both resistors.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Good start! Two resistors are in the loop, giving at least 1.5 kΩ of total resistance.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Place two resistors in the same series loop with the source. Increase their values so together they reach around 1.5 kΩ.

steps.instructionText: Adjust the pair to about 2 kΩ total so the divider only draws a few milliamps.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is under 5 mA — perfect for a light sensor input.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise the resistor values. More resistance in series lowers current for the same 9V source.

steps.instructionText: Make the lower resistor roughly double the upper one so most voltage drops there.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Your divider still sees the full 9V across both resistors, ready to split between them based on the ratio.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Keep the total at 9V and adjust the ratio. Remember: in a series divider, the source voltage is shared across both resistors.

questions.questionText: Why should a voltage divider use fairly large resistors when feeding a sensor?
options: Large resistors keep current low and waste less power
Large resistors make voltage readings more accurate by themselves
questions.explanation: High resistance keeps divider current small so you don't waste power or overload a sensor input.

questions.questionText: If both divider resistors are equal, what happens?
options: All voltage drops on the first resistor
All voltage drops on the second resistor
questions.explanation: Equal resistors share voltage equally in series.
